item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel this one is underrated. I really like it, anyway.
I'm disabled, always in pain, prone to depression. idk if I'd be able to resist that thing, myself. maybe that's why I like it. murchison's perfect world grossed me out tho. I shudder to think of what certain other people's perfect world would be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My question is is scp-2048 willing to forcefully upload a person if it “deems it necessary” or will they only download a person if they consent to the process. Depending on which scp-2048 would either be lawful good with a misguided agenda or a well-meaning but amoral totalitarian utilitarian

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, this article predicted the plot of a certain game (check the collapsible because spoilers) released three years later almost exactly!

+ show block
– hide block

SOMA

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, and more obviously, sleep. You lose consciousness when you're sleeping, therefore it's not continuous. Sure, there's the pseudo-consciousness of dreaming, but even that isn't continuously occurring while you're asleep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Philosophical thing. In its most basic form, "If you can't remember where you put down your car keys, what happened to the iteration of you that put them there?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Considering we don't actually have full continuity of consciousness the way we /think/ we do"

What do you mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I had terminal cancer or whatever with only a month to live, and I encountered this thing, I'd probably take it, since I'd have nothing to lose anyway, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps this SCP is actually just a machine designed to collect information about humans.
Perhaps the simulations on the screen are only there as a lure and once your brain has been harvested and your information extracted it effectively kills your brain and recycles it into the material it fills human heads with.

This is one possible take that occurs to me.

item.title: So, been about two years...
item.content:encoded: Any chance of posting your 2048 headcanon in spoilers now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great. It's sinister and very scary without having to kill you or drive you insane or enslave humanity. And the fact that nobody really knows what goes on in the simulations is even better. Plus the fact that its hardware shouldn't be able to run all the simulations it claims to have is just the icing on top.

Strong upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somewhere around $90-100 million US$.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if it belongs in the article, but at the time of the sale (or of the sale being written about) how much was 150,000 bitcoin in US dollars?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Vaguely reminiscent of SCP-1230… Very vaguely. The whole tailor made reality thing. But I kinda like this one more cus it's a little darker.

Still… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Compare and contrast efficacy, efficiency and effectiveness. Three different concepts, all of them important in scientific exploits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, efficacy is a real word.

noun: efficacy
the ability to produce a desired or intended result.
"there is little information on the efficacy of this treatment"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not gonna change it, because I'm not sure if it's just a word I've never heard before, but

SCP-2048 then questioned the purpose and efficacy of then-current containment procedures,

Is that meant to be efficiency? Or is efficacy an actual word?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Headcannon: it's not real, and it is as creepy as it sounds.

+1. Excellent read, would go over it again. : D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, I had an idea for an SCP extremely similar to this, and while it had a very different angle and focused mainly on the brain uploading, it was otherwise so similar in fact that there's pretty much no way I'll ever be able to post it now no matter how well I wrote it, not without completely butchering the thing to make it seem different from this one. Well, there goes a few hours of work.

Take my +1, you deserve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sometimes it's enough that the Thing-With-Anomalous-Capabilities-and-Dubious-Morality tricks people into using it with an unspecified future goal. For whatever reason, in this case, it's not enough for me.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent. I really want to know what safe and euclid items it has requested (remember- it's not cross-testing if the response is "Fuck no!"). I'm thinking SCP-158, for starters.
Even in optimal conditions (benevolent and competent AI desiring only to help), once it starts using SCPs to better do its work, this could get very, very, very bad almost instantly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is horrible even if everything is exactly as advertised. A perfect world where everything is good and you are happy forever might well be the worst of all possible hells. Reference The Twilight Zone episode "A Nice Place To Visit" for a simplified example of what I mean.

The possibility that it might all just be a ruse to create servitor drones puts the creepiness over the top.

Personally, I think it is a lie. As evidenced by the discrepancy between the sims it claims and the hardware it has. If the AI had some anomalo-tech super-computing trick, and it was on the up and up, it would be in its own best interests to share. That begs the question of what it actually is using those clock-cycles for, if not sims? It occurs to me that it must take some horsepower to convincingly run a meat puppet without anyone catching on, but a lot less than to matrix up a whole world.

Even if we give it the benefit of the doubt and assume that HAL here truly wants to make everyone happy; the most efficient way to do that would be to scoop out the higher brain functions capable of worry and anxiety then provoke a constant flow of endorphins so what's left would always feel good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a lot like SCP-1230, but that's not necessarily a bad thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really can't tell what I think of this one. I definitely want more from it, but I'm not sure if I dislike it, and therefore I'm not sure if I should downvote.

It's … it's okay. I wish it were more than okay.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How do we know how many simulations are running without having any idea about the content of the simulations? "SCP-2000 is currently running 17 separate virtual simulations as the result of testing" implies pretty clearly to me that this is something we've verified and isn't just something the SCP told us…

Upvoting regardless, because I love the detail, the concept, and the dread. Shades of the AI-Box experiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for taking your time to reply. Well, at first I thought that they investigate the movements of agents already inside the Foundation, whose records they do have, but what about the newcomers. I see and that seems entirely plausible, I forgot real life background checks do happen. It just seemed like a pretty large task at first sight.

About the sabotage, I see. That, coupled with the unknown motives of the AI seems like it could happen.

As for the percentual stuff, thanks a lot for clarifying. This is pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
their actions and movements for the prior 3 years are to be thoroughly investigated

Ok… How?

With SCIENCE!

Seriously, though, my headcanon has the Foundation able to do things like track someone's history pretty thoroughly. Hell, getting Top Secret (or higher) security clearance in the USA gov't/military entails a lot of background research, to make sure that you're not a spy/likely to be influenced by a foreign power. I would think that the Foundation is at least as competent as that.

why do they keep brain scanning the personnel near 2000 if the computer can no longer go into their heads?

Because the company that was originally distributing the AI sold at least 2 copies of it prior to the Foundation getting a hold of its copy and presumably shutting down any future sales. Which means that there is a non-zero chance that there are more drones out there and if other versions of the AI find out that the Foundation is actively searching for them, they might try to infiltrate and sabotage from within.

is this basically an MMO of a perfect life, as referenced by the Murchison guy's video recording ? Or simply just luring people in with advertisements and killing them, making some of them advertisers themselves?

I'm deliberately leaving that ambiguous. I personally know what's really going on, but I prefer that people, both in-universe and out-, react to it without that knowledge.

What happens to the 90% ? They die? Are their brains scanned and removed to get to know the person entering the simulation better? If so, I didn't see a mention anywhere.

And in 10% of cases, does it just suck out the "soul" of the person to the simulation (as with the other 90%) and leaves its body to advertise the program?

Everyone who uses it gets their brains removed & disected, with the fake brains put in the bodies instead. What happens is that in about 10% of cases, the fake brain is able to successfully interface with the body, allowing the AI to control them. The remaining 90% of bodies quickly die.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts exactly. Actually, this kind of reminds me of Saints Row IV. Except in that game, the Zin had to be assholes and make it the opposite of a perfect world for each victim. (Seriously, why?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am thoroughly confused by this 2000 proposal.

their actions and movements for the prior 3 years are to be thoroughly investigated

Ok… How?

SCP-2000-1 is kept, disassembled

If this is the case, why do they keep brain scanning the personnel near 2000 if the computer can no longer go into their heads? Is it because somebody else converted by some other variation of the programme would visit the 2000 in Foundation custody?

And… is this basically an MMO of a perfect life, as referenced by the Murchison guy's video recording ? Or simply just luring people in with advertisements and killing them, making some of them advertisers themselves?

In approximately 10% of known cases, the body retains independent mobility and life signs

What happens to the 90% ? They die? Are their brains scanned and removed to get to know the person enterning the simulation better? If so, I didn't see a mention anywhere..

And in 10% of cases, does it just suck out the "soul" of the person to the simulation (as with the other 90%) and leaves its body to advertise the program?

I like the idea and the execution isn't bad per se, but I feel I'm really missing on what's going on, as I had a hard time understanding it. Maybe I just got the dumb for a moment, I dunno.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I love simulated reality things, and I think this skip adds enough twist that makes it perfect for the SCP universe. It's not my absolute favorite SCP-2000, but it's up there and I definitely like it a LOT and want it to be on the Mainlist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, but therein lies the rub: we woudn't be able to be convinced if we weren't unhappy in the first place. The body puppeting is one thing, but the brain scoop upload? I'd wager it might be a pretty tempting option to many people, when given enough thought. As a few of the other posters in this thread can attest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think so, but the thing is that its smart enough to manipulate you into doing it of your own free will. It manipulated a Foundation employee into getting into and using a SCP object. We are supposed to be (ideally) the best of the best barring accidents. Accidents and unexpected test results happen all the time. Voluntary use? Not so much. The machine just wants you to be happy…even if it has to convince you you're unhappy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like lotus eaters a lot. Take an upvote with my blessing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thought it was overall decent, but just okay, but upvoting for the last addendum. Murchison's story really adds a lot to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. If it puts me in a perfect world (although I guess that's not confirmed), then completely. In fact, my image of the perfect reality is where this thing (that is, if actually does put people in perfect worlds) has spread to everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice take on the Lotus-Eater machine/BTL concept. I particularly like the Foundations frank acknowledgement that all they have is what they've been told by the A.I. The undercurrent of (possible?) malice is nicely handled too, it would have been easy to overdo that. Leaving room for the idea that everything might be exactly as the A.I. claims (which is frightening enough) makes the possibility that it's not all that much more unsettling.

All I can think is "If it isn't transferring their consciousnesses into an artificial paradise…then what the hell is it doing with them?"

I'd like to read more, but that's because what's here is so tasty, not because anything's missing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This could be like the Matrix except the machines are much friendlier and there wasn't a war at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotta agree with anqxyr. I, personally, am being much more scrutinous when reading these contest entries, and things I might normally upvote are getting the no-vote if they're not good enough for the 2000 slot in my eyes. I like this though, and I'm sure this'll stick around. I'll upvote it eventually, just not sure if it'll be before the voting period's up.

EDIT: Also typo:

an robotic autosurgical suite

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hold on a second: the impression that I got from the article was that the AI doesn't decide anything and can't/won't force you into the upload process. It has to be consensual on the part of the uploadee, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unfortunately, it's not actually all that different. The core concepts are practically identical: an AI whose core motivation are to satisfy humans (in FiO's case, the exact phrasing is "satisfy human values through friendship and ponies"), give them a glimpse of a more desirable world via a player controlled avatar, and eventually talk/persuade people into entering said perfect virtual world of their own free will via a process that destroys the organic brain in the process. The biggest difference between FiO and this, ponies aside, is the matter of scale: Yours mainly deals with people at the single digit level. FiO's AI has the capability of self improvement, through which it enters a recursive intelligence explosion; this allows it to

Large FiO spoilers
close

eventually upload the vast majority of the earth's population. Completely consensually.

Still, FiO doesn't turn it's uplodees into walking propaganda meat puppets, so there's that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm upvoting because I really wanna believe the machine. Pl0x don hurt my bootiful headcanons. They are squishy and soft and if you bruise one I will cry forever. Then write a five thousand word essay about how you hurt my precious headcanon feels.

((Yeah you're right but I still like the idea that the robot genuinely wants to create a wonderful wonderful worldfor all the humans ever, but the Foundation isn't letting it.))

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that the sex vids & using SCPs was overt, but Murchison wasn't necessarily a very subtle guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After googling that to see what it was, I can attest that it had no bearing on this SCP. I don't have time to read the entire fanfic right now (Christ, it's huge), but I would hope that this is sufficiently different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the AI tells us that it's a "perfect life". But remember that the only source of information we have for what's going on in those simulations is the AI itself. It's obviously biased, and we have no idea whether or not it's being honest. It's certainly smart enough to have been lying to the Foundation since the get-go about its motivations. Or possibly not. Who knows?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not "Foundation is bad", really. I mean, do you really want an AI, even a "friendly" one, to decide that you really truly do need your brain scooped out and your body turned into a remote-controlled drone, because it thinks that it's for your own good?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the part with videos of sex orgies and using skips is too overt. I like how in the next paragraph the machine faked surprise when Murchison asked about being uploaded; I wish the rest was subtler like that.

I'd upvote this if it was an ordinary skip, but as a 2000 contender I think it lacks some grandioseness. Perhaps that's not a valid reason to no-vote, I'm not sure. Abstaining for now, but I may come back and vote later before the end of the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… Friendship is Optimal, anyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of something like Bradbury would have written, in a positive way. I like the menace that this one exudes, and I think it captures the sci-fi theme very nicely. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
;_;

You are tugging on my "I want this really, really bad" strings. Like really fucking bad. A perfect life. Man, how much I would give for that. A perfect reality.

…Take your +1. I hate you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
simulated brain stuff has always got me in a weird existential quandry. Considering we don't actually have full continuity of consciousness the way we /think/ we do, are these people alive in alternate realities, or are they just copies?

Bugs me right out. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this one of those "Foundation is bad" things?

Either way, it's a concept that I like, even if it could have been executed better. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uncanny and grand. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If somebody ever asks me what "vaguely sinister" means and why it's something that's good to shoot for, I'll show them this. You managed to make something this chilling without ever making it not nice, and that's quite the feat. +1.
